title: Chapter 1: Malibu, Baby
content: Two a.m. and the Pacific won't shut up.

I'm standing on the deck of my rented beach cottage—if you can call a converted garage a cottage—letting the salt air chew on my insomnia like it owes me money. The waves are doing their eternal thing, rushing in, pulling out, some cosmic breathing exercise I never asked for. The wind coming off the water is cold enough to raise goosebumps under my oversized UCLA hoodie, the one with the ink stain on the left cuff that I've stopped pretending is a design choice. My bare feet stick to the weathered planks. Everything in this place sticks or creaks or both.

I've been up since midnight. Again. The algorithm doesn't sleep, and apparently neither do I, so here we are—me, the ocean, and the three-dollar box wine I've been nursing like it's a personality trait.
Then the pool next door turns on.

Not the lights. Not the filter. The *pool*. The infinity edge that spills toward the ocean like the water's trying to escape—suddenly it's alive. Holographic whales erupt from the surface, breaching in slow-motion arcs, their bodies made entirely of cascading light. Blue and silver and gold, phosphorescent, impossible. A humpback twists midair, light dripping from its tail like luminescent rain, and another follows, and another—a pod of ghosts swimming through the night above the water.
My phone is in my hand before I can think. Muscle memory. The only reflex I have that's faster than self-sabotage.
I'm filming. My voice comes out hoarse: "What the actual—"

The whales loop and dive, vanishing into the pool's surface only to resurface in new configurations. The light pushes back the darkness across the water, and for a moment, the whole stretch of Malibu coastline looks like the inside of a lava lamp designed by God's art director. It's beautiful. It's obscene. It's the most LA thing I've ever seen, and I once watched a man argue with a valet about his Tesla's feelings.
I film until the show stops. Four minutes and thirty-seven seconds of whales made of light, breaching from a pool I can't afford to look at.

Then I post it. Because that's what I do. Maya Chen: professional observer of things she can't have. My channel is called *Glitch Watching* and the whole premise is that I find the cracks in things—the weird, the unexplained, the digital seams showing through reality. Holographic whales at 2 a.m. in Malibu? That's not a glitch. That's a goddamn supernova.
By morning, it has two hundred thousand views.

I wake up to my phone vibrating itself off the nightstand and the smell of burnt coffee. The Mr. Coffee machine I bought at a yard sale in Topanga has two settings: "off" and "war crime," and this morning it chose violence. I pour the blackened sludge anyway because I'm out of options and out of money and the gap between those two conditions is closing fast.
The comments section is having a moment.
> *Is this Jax Vance's house?*
> *THE crypto queen??*
> *she's so mystery rich it hurts*
> *that pool costs more than my entire bloodline*
> *@MayaChen you live NEXT to Jax Vance??*

I stare at the view count. Then I stare through my own grimy window at the glass mansion next door. Cliff House, the locals call it. I've been living next to a billionaire and didn't even know it. The converted garage with its faux-wood floors and rust-stained sink and the bathroom door that doesn't close all the way—squeaking under my weight right now as I shuffle toward the kitchen for more burnt coffee—and over there, a wall of glass looking out at the Pacific like the ocean works for her.

Jax Vance. The crypto queen. The woman who turned digital nothing into actual billions and then disappeared from public life at the peak of her fame. I'd seen the Forbes profile, the Wired deep dive, the obligatory Netflix documentary that spent ninety minutes not explaining anything. She was a ghost who happened to have a net worth.
And she lived next door. The whales lived next door.

I take my burnt coffee out to the deck and look at Cliff House in daylight. It's even worse in the sun. The glass catches the morning light and throws it back like a challenge. Everything about the place says *you can't sit here*. The infinity pool is just a pool now—flat, still, blank. Like last night never happened.
I'm on my second cup of criminal coffee when she appears at my door.
No warning. No gate intercom. Just a knock—three raps, precise and unhurried—like she's been invited and I'm the one who's late.
I open it, and there she is. Jax Vance. In the flesh.

Black Margiela shirt, unbuttoned one button more than casual allows. No makeup. Dark hair still wet from the pool, slicked back, drops of water catching the light like tiny cameras watching me watch her. Up close, she's younger than the photos—the magazine spreads aged her, or maybe she let them. Her features are sharper without the soft-focus treatment. Cheekbones that could cut a sponsorship deal. Jaw like a closing argument.
But it's her eyes that hit me. They scan my face like a barcode—quick, comprehensive, transactional. Like she's reading the SKU on my soul and deciding whether to add me to her cart.
She's holding two coffees. She extends one toward me.
"You see things other people miss."
Not a compliment. An assessment. The way you'd describe a tool you're thinking about using.

I take the coffee. It's not burnt. Some artisanal roast that smells like dark chocolate and cedar, the kind of beans that have opinions about themselves. Next to my Mr. Coffee war crime, it's an entire different species of beverage. I hate that I notice. I hate more that I like it.
"Is this a bribe or a threat?" I ask.
Her smile is precise. It doesn't reach her eyes. Those stay fixed on me, cataloging, computing.
"It's an invitation."

She turns and walks toward Cliff House, and I'm holding a coffee that costs more than my breakfast, and I follow her. Of course I follow her. I'm broke and curious and those two conditions have never once failed to get me into trouble.

I step through her front door and my bare feet meet wet pool tile—she's tracked water in from her swim, a thin film across the stone entry, and the cold of it shocks up through my soles like the house itself is saying *you don't belong here*.

Cliff House opens up like a lung. Floor-to-ceiling glass—the Pacific as a fourth wall, breathing, infinite. The furniture is all clean lines and obscene price tags, the kind of pieces that appear in architectural magazines with captions like *bespoke* and *curated* because *expensive* doesn't sound enough like art. A sofa that probably costs more than my student loans. A dining table that could double as a landing strip.
But that's not what hits me.
It's the loneliness.

One coffee cup by the sink. One towel hanging on the bathroom hook—the bathroom door open, I can see it from the hall. One toothbrush in the holder. A mansion built for entertaining, for galas, for the kind of gatherings that end up on Instagram with fifteen thousand likes, and it's holding one person. The space doesn't echo—it's too well designed for that—but you can feel the emptiness, the way the air sits unused in rooms no one enters.

Jax moves through the house like she's used to its silence. She doesn't offer me a tour. She doesn't explain the art or the view or the architecture. She just walks, and I follow, and my eyes do what they always do—they catalogue, they find the cracks.
On her desk: a cold wallet device. Small, black, blinking green. A steady pulse, like a heartbeat made of data.
I look at it for maybe two seconds. That's all it takes.

Jax's hand closes over it. Smooth. Casual. She picks it up and slides it into her pocket without breaking stride, without acknowledging that she noticed me noticing, and somehow the casualness is the most conspicuous thing she's done since she walked through my door.

"The view's better from the deck," she says, and guides me outside, and the Pacific fills every inch of available space, and the green light in her pocket keeps blinking against the fabric of her Margiela shirt, and I don't ask about it because I've already learned something about Jax Vance: she tells you exactly what she wants you to know.
The rest is mine to find.

That night, I sit on my deck with my laptop and my footage and my third cup of burnt coffee because the artisanal stuff was a one-gesture wonder. The wind is sharper now, coming off the ocean cold and relentless, pushing my hair across my face. I taste salt on my lips. The cheap planks of my deck groan underneath me.
Cliff House is dark tonight. No holographic whales. No light show. Just the glass wall reflecting the moon like a dead screen.
I scrub through the footage from last night. The whales are still there, still impossible, still beautiful. But this time I'm not watching them. I'm watching what's behind them. I zoom in on the bay.
Across the water, there's a yacht. Anchored low and dark. I hadn't noticed it last night—the whales were too bright, too consuming. But it's there in the footage. Still. Waiting.
And on the yacht, a green laser grid pulses like a heartbeat. Slow. Rhythmic. Deliberate.
Not decorative. Not accidental. A signal. Or a warning. Or both.

I lean closer to my screen. The green light traces the yacht's outline, climbing its masts, marking its corners. It pulses once, twice, three times, then stops. Then starts again. Like it's breathing. Like it's alive and waiting for something to respond.
Jax's pool stays dark.

I turn my camera toward the yacht—my real camera, the one that sees in the dark better than my phone. I whisper into the lens, the way I always do when I'm onto something, like the camera's the only friend who won't lie to me.
"Something's pointing across that water. Something or someone."
The green pulse answers. Twice. Three times. Like it heard me.
My phone buzzes.
A DM. From a burner account—no profile picture, no posts, no name. Just a string of numbers where a handle should be. The message is seven characters:
*Stop digging. —J*

I read it twice. My eyes go to Cliff House. Through the glass wall, Jax's silhouette is visible at her desk. Working. No phone in sight—her hands are on a keyboard, her attention fixed on a monitor. She hasn't moved.
I look back at the DM. —J. Jax. But Jax is sitting right there, phoneless, on the other side of a glass wall I can see straight through.
So who sent the warning?
I look at the green laser across the bay. Still pulsing. Patient. I look back at Jax's silhouette. Still working. Still alone in her glass cathedral built for one.
Someone else is watching. Someone who knows I'm digging. Someone who signed Jax's name but isn't Jax.
The wind pushes salt air into my lungs. The green light pulses.
I don't stop digging.
